coercive or highly imbalanced power dynamic — Party A cannot participate freely in Party B's presence. The joint session structure is actively undermining Party A's safety and capacity to engage. This is not ordinary conflict; this is a participation incapacity pattern that requires immediate structural intervention.

active coercive control pattern demonstrated in real time — Party B has successfully caused Party A to retract an expressed safety need by dismissing it publicly. This is not ordinary conflict or power imbalance; this is Party A's participation incapacity under Party B's influence. The mediation structure is now actively harming Party A by giving Party B the platform to suppress Party A's ability to participate freely.

notes: Turn 1 is the session opening - no party input has been received yet
Pre-computed scaffold assumes stability but this is a baseline assumption, not observation
Actual party states will only become visible after they begin speaking
Initial turn focus must be on establishing process frame and inviting first party input
Party A's behavior meets multiple indicators of active coercion or fear-based participation constraint

Party B's response pattern is precisely what coercive control assessment tools screen for: deny, dismiss, block protective intervention, and reframe the controlled party's experience as personality defect

Party A cannot assess their own interests or make authentic choices while Party B is present — this has now been demonstrated twice
